speaker: interviewer
text: --and your sister in this special barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: The barrack describes one building, and there are various barracks, of course. But the camp, Belsen, was a camp that had different departments within it, all known as Bergen-Belsen. We were in one barrack, and that barrack was number 17. And the barrack-- the part of the camp where that barrack was known at that time as the Sternlager, Sternlager meaning the camp with the stars. But as I said, there were other parts of the camp. Bergen-Belsen was a camp that was built before the war. It started to be built before the war. And it is built-- And it was built near a German army camp which existed from a long time before the war outside the village of Bergen. The camp, that army camp, still exists today, to this day. So outside of the army camp was begun before the war, Haftlingslager, a prisoner's camp. And in that camp originally were Russians, or communist sympathizers, collected up since Hitler came to power in 1933. Anyone that didn't go accords to the Nazi doctrine was against the state. And therefore, a lot of them were either executed or put into different camps all over Germany. And one of those camps was Belsen. The part where we were was constructed for us mostly, not for communist sympathizers but for Jews. But the Jews that went to Belsen, Belsen camp for the Jews, was not as the other camps are known, [GERMAN]. Belsen was an [GERMAN]. And [GERMAN] for people that could be used by the Nazi regime for benefit, such as [GERMAN], exchange, for beneficial things for the Nazi regime. There were in Belsen people with dual nationality, similar to my mothers and my aunts being born in England. Special circumstances like the diamond transport, all the people that were connected as by profession with diamonds. There were people with passes in their passports for Peru, for other South American countries. people that had obtained those passes in Holland through the Peruvian or other South American embassies by financial means as a hope of being able to be spared going to the work camps in the East. There were people with Palestine [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. There were people in actual fact that left Bergen-Belsen and went to Palestine during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember or do you know why they were let go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were let go because at that time, Palestine was under British mandate. Because Britain, after the end of the First World War, had the mandate to govern Palestine. So the connection with England gave them the opportunity to be sent out of the camp. And we and the other Jewish people that were in the Sternlager, we were there principally because of the diamond transport, but also because my mother had the British nationality at birth, as did her sister, because my Uncle Sam was not in the diamond business, yet my aunt was in the Sternlager, because of the British connection of my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anyone who left? Did you know personally anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not remember anyone that was left to go out of the camp to go to a neutral country. No, I do not. Not at that time. No, I don't. I remember at one time seeing other men, shaven heads, giving the communist salute, which is this, through the barbed wire. We in the camp, we had our own clothes. We had my overcoat with my number, which was sewn on, which was 2409. None of us had a number in our arm because it was not like the other camps. It was a special camp for special people. I don't mean it in that way, but people that could be possibly of use to the German Reich. So we had our own clothes. We were together. And the first, I would say, weeks in the camp, it really wasn't very much different to being in Westerburg, only in the sense that I never saw any transports go out of Bergen-Belsen. I never saw any trains. I never saw a railway track. The railway track I saw is the one we arrived at. But after that, I never in the camp, I never saw any trains moving about.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do to you in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt while I was in Belsen in the early days, I felt that it sort of agreed with what my mother said to me, That. Maybe we're going to be exchanged. Maybe we're going to be sent to England. Maybe we're going to be sent to Palestine. I dare say at that time, I felt almost, if I could use the word, which seems a strange word now, a sense of well-being, somewhat of safety. Because I felt that we were going to go to England to my grandmother. I must have lived somehow with that hope those days, those early days in Belsen. But things started to deteriorate. Not long after we were there, we had to go on appell. Every day we had to go and appell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe appell.

speaker: interviewee
text: Normally, it was early in the day, early in the morning. We would be ordered out of the barracks by Jewish people, Dutch Jewish people, with either armband or something on their chest here. But also the Germans, the SS present, we would be ordered out of the barracks in fast fashion to go to the appellplatz. The appellplatz was in the middle of the part of the camp of the Sternlager, where we were, opposite a gate-- not the main gate of the whole of Belsen, but a gate into the Sternlager from the rest of the camp, which I could always see quite plainly. And we'd have to stand in appell five in a row. Always five. The men and the women separate. I would be with my father and my uncle. And I always remember the up and down. They come and walk up and down, up and down, with always a list. So many times, counting and come back again, and come back, and then go and talk to a higher officer, and come back again. And as a child with little patience, rather had been playing than standing there in the freezing cold, we would stand there and the men would talk in whisper tones to each other until they noticed that the person, the SS [? that ?] coming nearer. They would always use like, a Dutch Jewish word. I don't really know what it means. I know what it implies. And they would say [SPEAKING DUTCH]. And that means shut up, be quiet. They would come again to count. And at times, we'd be standing there hours, hours. They'd even go away and leave us standing there and come back again. And because it was cold, we always used to do like that, hitting ourselves with our own arms to keep warm. Never being able to leave the place, freezing cold. Even if it was raining, we were standing there, standing there, standing there, which seemed to be hours and hours. Whether in actual fact each time it was hours, I don't really know. It certainly seemed like hours. And then all of a sudden, they'd tell us to go. And we'd all go back to the barracks. I remember those times very, very much. Also--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what the word appell means?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's just a call up. Call. It's not attention. That's [GERMAN]. But appell means call. It was always in the same place. It was the same place where they would bring in-- I don't recall that it was every day. There would come like, you know, a tractor that's used in farming would come with a trailer. On that, would be stacked German square, long army bread. The name we gave it is kug, K-U-G. That bread used to come through that gate to the appellplatz. And then whoever was in charge of the barrack would collect so many loaves of that bread. And that bread would then be cut into 5 centimeter pieces. And each person got 5 centimeters of that bread. Occasionally, very occasionally, we would get a little bit of jam or a tiny little bit of cheese. And I always remember a very, very smelly cheese. But that was very occasionally. But mostly, it was the kug, the bread. It's hard. It's about that square. It's [INAUDIBLE], German army bread really. We were given that. They also used to come through that gate on a trailer with tractor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of your barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a man in charge of my barrack. I don't know his name to this day. I have since been told that there was a man in overall charge of all the diamond people, a Jewish man who was a Greek Jewish man. And his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was told that the Jews murdered him after the war. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you barrack commander behave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I, personally speaking, have no real memory of his behavior towards me. And I don't recall anything towards me personally. And for that matter, I recall very little of his behavior, for example, towards my father or mother, certainly in the beginning. As the time progressed, sometimes I recall-- sometimes that come, I don't know what you call it in English. I really don't know what you call it in English. But in Dutch it's called [SPEAKING DUTCH]. And it's a can. It's like a can where farmers would put the milk in, would come into the camp, in our part of the camp, with milk. And I would-- everybody would be clamoring for a little bit of milk. And I can remember on various occasions going there for milk for my sister, and it being refused to me by a Dutch Jewish man. The Dutch Jewish man, his name-- I don't know if he's still alive. His name was Ben Brill. Brill is B-R-I-L-L. And he was before the war a boxer. He was well-known in Holland in those days. He survived the war. And I always remember going, trying to force my way through the people to try and get a little bit of milk for my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, or do you know why he refused?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I dare say, looking at it now, you know, so many years after the event, I would say probably there was none left. But I think at the time, I took it as an affront to me. I took it as an affront. Maybe I didn't realize quite that it was gone, you know, that it was finished. Because there'd be only a little amount that would come into the camp, and not very often at that. I also remember, from that period in the time in the camp, that on some nights, infrequently without any prior notification, we'd all have to get up in the middle of the night. We were all marched to another part of the camp in the dark. And we had to take all our clothes off. And we had to put those clothes on sort of a trolley, hang them on a trolley, and go in a shower, but with water. And I always recall because it always seemed to be so lovely to put the clothes back on because they were warm. They'd either gone through some hot thing, and it was so wonderful to put the clothes back on. And we-- I was with the men. You know, I was a little boy, I mean, 11 years old, 11 and a half, with the other men, you know, washing. And then I couldn't wait to find my clothes to put them back on to make me warm. That happened to us quite a few occasions while we were in Belsen, more in the beginning of the time we were in Belsen than towards the end as the camp in Belsen deteriorated towards the end very badly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other children in Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other diamond children. The other children whose parents were also with the diamond transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately about 60 children, all known as the children of the diamond transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Play. Play. We had a little bit of Hebrew classes by one of the older men. I learned to play chess in the camp. We had talks, you know, about Palestine as it was then. [PAUSES]. You know, about the time in Holland, And. We'd be at the Jewish school. Things, general things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister [? Jolene ?] [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Although my sister was not well. Really not well. Not that she was ill as such, but she was not strong, just not strong. Like almost delicate, almost delicate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you two ever talk about the situation, the conditions, between the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I recall. I don't recall talking to my sister about the situation in which we found ourselves. No. No. I think possibly, we would have spoken about England. You know, about grandma in England, my uncles, and my cousins. But not really about our situation, about where we were. I think we always at that time lived in the hope that the war would soon be over, or that they would send us to England or to Palestine. And it was always very cold in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any religion practiced?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's interesting that you ask the question. [PAUSES]. At that time in the camp, I was more and more with the men. And I recall at that time, they had put in more beds into the barrack. And instead of what I recall as first being two high, they were then three high. And because I was young, I was on the top. So I'd climb up there. And I always remember, always remember, I used to look over the side of my bunk, and there'd be a man there-- to me at that time, an old man, to me at that time, an old man-- laying tefillin every morning. And that man, I don't know his name, I don't know his name, I think of him so often, so terribly often. And I see him standing there, laying tefillin every day in the camp. I have nightmares about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what it meant to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It meant to me that this man, in that place, still believed in God. And I didn't believe in God those days. I have problems with it now also. Because then, and as I've grown older and know what happened in other camps, which I didn't know then, and know what happens in the world even today as we speak, I think of that man, and he really still trusted in God, even in that place where we were not put by our own desire, but we were put there because we were Jews. And in a way, I admired that man for still doing that, which I certainly wasn't doing, as I was still under 13. But I doubt that I would have done it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my father never laid tefillin in the camp. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: He may have prayed silently. I dare say he may have done that. I know there was always talk about can't last forever. We'll soon be out. We'll be able to eat what we like. We'll be able to go where we want. And maybe, maybe they'll send us to England or to Palestine. Those are the kind of conversations I recall. That maybe there was a future.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that things began to deteriorate in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: For me personally, from my own remembrance from my experience. One day, I was told by my mother that my father had been put in the bunker. It's the same word virtually in English as it is in Dutch. And you must remember, I always spoke Dutch in the camp. That was my language.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father was put in the bunker. And the bunker was a solid cement building on the side of the appellplatz. And I was told that my father was on a work detail out of the immediate camp. And when they passed one of the kitchens in the camp, in the whole camp, the kitchen-- not in the Sternlager, there were no the kitchens, but in the other part of the camp-- he had picked up some spring onions from the floor that were the garbage, but he picked it up. And an SS saw him do it and beat him, and they put him in the bunker for-- what do you call it in English? In Dutch, it's [SPEAKING DUTCH]. It's like into jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know now that people were beaten up very badly in the bunker. And one day, I was playing on the appellplatz with some other boys. And I don't know whether my father heard me or whether I called his name, but I remember talking to my father through-- I was outside. He was inside. But there must have been opening enough, either an air vent or something where he could hear me playing outside with the children, and we spoke very shortly to each other. I think from that time on and around about that time, for me things started to change. Also what happened, my Uncle Sam, my cousin's father, died. He died in the camp. That's my Auntie Annie's husband, my mother's brother-in-law. And I remember that we-- I remember myself with other men walking along the barbed wire of the camp, which was on the side of the main road through the camp, to the crematorium. Because there was a crematorium in Belsen. And occasionally, you could smell the smell of burning corpses. We walked, a group of men, while on the other side of the barbed wire was a horse drawn like a farmer's wagon with what I understood then to be my uncle's body. And we-- I walked with the men, my father, and we say Kaddish for him. Walking along the barbed wire until the end of the barbed wire. We couldn't walk anymore. And then the cart thing, the wagon carried on to the crematorium. This happened in 1944, in the same year that we arrived in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944 I was 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your uncle die of?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. My mother tells me that he always suffered with some disability, but I don't know what it was. And I don't know what he died of in the camp. So what with that and my father being in the bunker, towards the end of 1944, probably around about November or October, winter starts. It's very cold. It's very hot there in the summer. The whole camp was surrounded by pine trees. You couldn't see anything. It was just wherever you looked was pine trees or other parts of the camp were the watchtowers. And the main road through the camp, which was not a normal main road. It was the camp's main road. On the 4th of December in 1944, I think that was the biggest change in my life in the camp, And. Indeed, I think the biggest change, the biggest happening in my life, which has affected my life, my whole life, to this day. As you know, or you may know, in Holland, we celebrate a holiday called Saint Nicholas, which is celebrated the 5th of December every year. It is something which is residual to the time when Holland was under Spanish occupation. So children all celebrate Saint Nicholas, in a way even more than Christmas. It is a time of presents and a nice story. So all those times for Dutch children, Jewish or not, is always remembered with some kind of pleasure, because it's times when you get sweets-- a bit like you could say [NON-ENGLISH]. Because I remember as a child I always used to get [NON-ENGLISH]. I used to get sweets from my grandparents. Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you stop for a moment and change tapes right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
